followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Two substantive changes from cycle 1: (1) Added the LHY prescription issue (Eq. 15 complex for ε_dd > 1) as a new moderate technical objection — this is a valid point from codex that I missed. (2) Upgraded and sharpened the reciprocal-lattice/Bloch-band-projection objection: codex's observation that Eqs. (27)-(29) drop the m≠0 reciprocal-lattice vertex terms present in Eq. (11) without derivation is more precise than my prior framing and I now mark it major alongside the self-contradiction objection. Overall score revised from 6.2 to 5.8 and technical_soundness from 6 to 5.5. Verdict held at 'promising'.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex's LHY concern should not be rated 'major / would change verdict': the paper cites Lima & Pelster [43,44] who introduced the standard real-part prescription for dipolar BECs, and the cited supersolid reference [37] (Ilg & Büchler) uses the same framework. If the paper follows this convention implicitly, the concern is a clarity/reproducibility issue (moderate), not a computational error (fatal). This does not change the recommendation to require explicit statement.

Gemini's 'routine' verdict: the physical setting (polaron in a spontaneously symmetry-broken supersolid with band-structured Goldstone modes) is genuinely new and the identification of the divergence and localization regimes adds substantive physical content, even if the methodology is adapted from existing work. 'Promising' is more appropriate than 'routine'.

Codex's 'contested' verdict: 'contested' implies active disagreement among credible positions. The paper's limitations are acknowledged by the authors themselves; the debate is about whether those limitations are fatal to the qualitative conclusions, not about whether the results are controversial among the community. 'Promising' better captures a valid first exploration with known approximation failures.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex: LHY coefficient in Eq. (15) is complex for ε_dd > 1 and the paper does not state the real-part or analytic-continuation prescription used — a reproducibility gap even if the intended convention matches Lima-Pelster.

Codex: The path-integral polaronic energy equations (27)-(29) appear to integrate only over q in the first BZ using V_IB(q)^2, while the original vertex Eq. (11) contains V_IB(q + m k_s) summed over reciprocal-lattice index m. No derivation shows these m≠0 terms vanish under Bloch projection.

Gemini and Codex (both): The single-droplet localization prediction is self-contradictory within the stated low-momentum, lowest-band approximation — a spatially localized state requires broad momentum support. This confirms and sharpens my prior major objection.
Codex: Numerical reproducibility details (cutoffs for l, m, m', s sums; BdG bands retained in polaron sums; convergence tests near the transition) are incomplete.

main_claims.claim: A variational path-integral framework can be constructed for an impurity in a 1D dipolar supersolid within the Bogoliubov–Fröhlich approximation, reducing the problem to a Fröhlich-like vertex in a periodic background handled via an effective Bloch mass.
main_claims.notes: The formal derivation follows established lines, but the reduction from the full periodic-impurity Hamiltonian (Eq. 11, containing reciprocal-lattice sums) to the effective-mass free impurity (Eq. 13) is asserted rather than derived via a Bloch projection. It is unclear whether form factors and Umklapp components are legitimately dropped. The paper acknowledges the approximation is only valid at low momentum and for the lowest band.

main_claims.claim: The polaronic energy diverges at the superfluid-supersolid phase transition, driven by roton-mode softening.
main_claims.notes: Divergence is observed numerically and is qualitatively consistent with Ref. [32]. However, the Bogoliubov theory fails exactly here (large local depletion), so the magnitude cannot be trusted. The experimental-signature language around this divergence overstates what the controlled calculation supports.

main_claims.claim: For large enough impurity-atom scattering lengths, the polaron becomes localized to a single droplet, behaving like a small solid-state polaron.
main_claims.notes: The radius formula (Eq. 30) and effective Bloch mass are derived under the low-momentum, single-band approximation that is self-contradictory in the strong-coupling/large-mass regime where localization is claimed. Beyond-Fröhlich corrections are expected to be substantial here. The paper explicitly acknowledges these limitations but still presents localization as a headline result.

main_claims.claim: A finite impurity magnetic moment increases the polaronic energy and polaron radius, leading to a weaker polaron.
main_claims.notes: Effect is monotonic, physically intuitive, and consistent across all figures. Not dependent on the approximations that are most contested.

main_claims.claim: Perturbation theory and the variational path-integral approach give similar results in the supersolid regime for the parameters studied.
main_claims.notes: Explicit agreement shown in Fig. 3, validating the numerical implementation and confirming the weak-coupling regime for the chosen parameters.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The LHY correction for ε_dd > 1 uses a specific real-part or analytic-continuation prescription.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Without this prescription, Eq. (15) yields complex values in the supersolid regime studied. The LHY term is essential to stabilize the supersolid state and determines the BdG spectrum and all downstream polaron results.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Projecting the impurity onto the lowest Bloch band reduces the impurity-phonon vertex to V_IB(q) with q in the first BZ only, i.e., form factors and Umklapp contributions from higher reciprocal-lattice terms in Eq. (11) are negligible.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is the bridge between the derived supersolid Hamiltonian and all path-integral energy and radius formulas. If the reciprocal-lattice components are not small, the polaronic energy is systematically underestimated and the polaron radius miscalculated.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The Fröhlich approximation (linear impurity-excitation coupling, neglecting two-Bogoliubov-excitation vertex) is adequate for all parameter values shown, including the strong-coupling localization regime.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. II, text after Eq. (10); Sec. IV discussion of Fig. 5
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Beyond-Fröhlich corrections are known to be important at intermediate and strong coupling for other Bose polaron problems. The localization result sits in this regime and could be qualitatively altered.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The transverse Gaussian ansatz (Eq. 2) for both bosons and impurity remains valid for all impurity-atom interaction strengths considered, including those that localize the polaron.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. II, Eq. (2) and Eq. (4) discussion
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The authors acknowledge this breaks down for strong coupling, but no estimate of the critical coupling is given. The localization result requires strong coupling, making this limitation directly relevant to the paper's novel claim.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The variational memory-kernel iteration converges to the global minimum of the Feynman-Jensen upper bound for all parameter values, not a local one biased by the Lee-Low-Pines initialization.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Sec. IV, text describing iterative procedure
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: For the band-structured supersolid problem, which is more complex than the single-mode BEC polaron, multiple local minima are plausible. No initialization sensitivity check is reported.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Three order parameters (modes m = 0, ±1) are sufficient to describe the supersolid wave function throughout the parameter range studied.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The truncation is stated but not validated for stronger dipolar interactions deeper in the supersolid regime. Higher harmonics could affect the BdG spectrum and Fröhlich coupling constants.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the lowest-Bloch-band projection of the impurity Hamiltonian (Eq. 11) correctly reduce the impurity-phonon vertex to V_IB(q) with q in the first BZ only, or do Bloch form factors and reciprocal-lattice transfers (m≠0 terms) remain? Specifically, is the path-integral kernel in Eqs. (27)-(29) the correct lowest-band projection of Eq. (11)?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The explicit matrix elements of the Bloch projection are not given in the paper, the equations in the HTML source have equation content rendered as empty tables, and auditing this requires working through the Fourier decomposition of the impurity density operator in the Bloch basis — a multi-step algebraic calculation.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: What LHY prescription is used for ε_dd > 1 in Eq. (15), and is it the same as the standard Lima-Pelster real-part convention applied to the quasi-1D effective potential rather than the 3D potential?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The paper does not state the prescription; resolving this requires either contacting the authors or reading their numerical implementation. The quasi-1D effective potential changes the integrand relative to the 3D case, so the convention is not automatically inherited.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the normalization condition for the Bogoliubov coefficients (Eq. 9) correct for the multi-mode supersolid case, where modes at k and k+G for reciprocal lattice vectors G are mixed? The standard BdG normalization for periodic systems involves a sum over bands.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The equation content is not readable from the HTML source, and verifying the normalization requires the full matrix structure of the BdG problem.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the Bloch mass calculation in the Appendix use the correct periodic potential — the impurity-boson interaction g_IB times the condensate density — or some other form? And is the band-gap large enough relative to the LHY-corrected dispersion that the single-band approximation is quantitatively reasonable even at the interaction strengths shown in Fig. 6?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Eq. (31) and the surrounding matrix equation are not readable from the HTML source, and quantitative band-gap estimation requires numerical evaluation of the specific potential depths.

novelty_assessment: The polaron problem in a dipolar supersolid has not been previously studied, making this the first application of Feynman path-integral polaron theory to a medium with a band-structured excitation spectrum arising from spontaneous translational symmetry breaking. This is a genuine gap in the literature. The methodological contributions are incremental — the authors adapt rather than invent techniques — but the combination of BdG-solved supersolid excitations with the general-memory-kernel path integral is non-trivial. Gemini's 'routine' characterization undersells the novelty of the physical setting; 'promising' is more accurate. The localization and divergence predictions, even if not quantitatively reliable within the current approximations, identify physically interesting regimes for future exact calculations.
score_rationale: The paper opens a genuinely new problem (polarons in dipolar supersolids) with established and largely appropriate methods. Novelty (7) is real and the writing is clear (7). Technical soundness (5, revised down from 6) is limited by three compounding issues: (1) the reduction from the reciprocal-lattice-structured vertex (Eq. 11) to the first-BZ-only path-integral kernel (Eqs. 27-29) is not derived — codex's sharpened framing of this gap is persuasive; (2) the self-contradictory use of the low-momentum/single-band approximation to predict strong-coupling localization is the most visible logical flaw; and (3) the LHY prescription for ε_dd > 1 is unstated, creating a reproducibility gap. Significance (6) reflects that the qualitative physics (transition divergence, droplet localization regime) is interesting and will motivate follow-up work, but the quantitative results in the contested regimes cannot be trusted at face value. The score 5.8 reflects a solid first exploration of new territory — honest about limitations but with derivation gaps that should be resolved before the numerical conclusions are accepted as quantitatively reliable.
summary: This paper applies the Feynman variational path-integral polaron formalism to a one-dimensional dipolar supersolid, computing the impurity ground-state energy and radius within the Bogoliubov and Fröhlich approximations. The main results are a divergence at the superfluid-supersolid transition (attributed to roton softening) and a predicted localization of the polaron to single droplets at strong impurity-atom coupling. The physical setting is genuinely novel. My cycle-2 assessment incorporates two sharpened concerns from other reviewers: (1) the LHY coefficient in Eq. (15) has an imaginary part for ε_dd > 1, which requires a real-part or analytic-continuation prescription that is not stated in the paper; and (2) the path-integral coupling sums in Eqs. (27)-(29) appear to drop reciprocal-lattice momentum components present in the original vertex (Eq. 11), and the paper does not derive this reduction from a proper Bloch-band projection. Both concerns are credible and sharpen my existing major objection about the Bloch mass approximation. I hold the 'promising' verdict over 'contested' or 'routine': the problem is genuinely new and the physics qualitatively plausible, but the derivation has real gaps that require author clarification or additional intermediate steps before numerical results can be trusted quantitatively.

technical_objections.evidence: Codex reviewer independently identified the same discrepancy. The paper asserts 'only the lowest-energy band contributes and the low-momentum limit can be taken' but does not show this reduces the vertex to the m=0 term alone.
technical_objections.objection: The reduction from the full periodic impurity Hamiltonian (Eq. 11) to a free impurity with effective Bloch mass (Eq. 13) is not derived. Eq. (11) contains the vertex V_IB(q + m k_s) exp[i(q + m k_s) z_I] summed over reciprocal-lattice index m; the path-integral polaronic energy equations (27)-(29) appear to use only q in the first Brillouin zone with V_IB(q)^2, implicitly setting m=0. No selection rule, Bloch form-factor calculation, or Wannier-function argument is supplied to justify dropping the reciprocal-lattice components.

technical_objections.evidence: Standard treatment in 3D dipolar BECs (Lima & Pelster [43,44], cited in the paper) takes the real part. The quasi-1D system has a different effective potential, so direct application of that prescription requires explicit justification. The omission is a reproducibility and correctness issue even if the intended prescription is standard.
technical_objections.objection: The LHY correction coefficient in Eq. (15) contains [1 – ε_dd + 3ε_dd u²]^(5/2), which is negative near u=0 for ε_dd > 1. The paper uses ε_dd values above 1 (e.g., critical around 1.34–1.345 per Fig. 1) without stating whether a real-part convention (Lima–Pelster prescription) or analytic continuation is used. The LHY term enters the ground-state energy, chemical potential, and BdG spectrum that underlie all polaron results.

technical_objections.evidence: Paper states 'Bogoliubov theory does not describe the small polaron limit' and 'the localization of the polaron happens in the intermediate- or strong-coupling regime where beyond-Fröhlich corrections probably have a substantial effect.' Both gemini and codex reviewers independently raised the same self-contradiction.
technical_objections.objection: The treatment of the periodic background via a single effective Bloch mass (Eq. 13) is simultaneously justified as a 'low-momentum limit' while the polaron-localization result (Fig. 5) is claimed to demonstrate single-droplet hopping. These are self-contradictory: a spatially localized polaron requires broad momentum support, violating the low-momentum approximation. The paper acknowledges this but still presents localization as a key result.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. II, Eq. (13); Sec. IV, Fig. 5 discussion

technical_objections.evidence: No quantitative check of wave-function positivity is reported for the stronger-interaction results. Violation would affect the LHY energy and hence the BdG input to the polaron calculation.
technical_objections.objection: The positivity assumption on the condensate wave function (Eq. 16), needed to drop the absolute value in the LHY energy, is only stated to be 'valid close to the superfluid-supersolid transition.' Results are shown for parameter values well into the supersolid regime (Figs. 3, 5, 6) where large density modulations make positivity plausible but unverified.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper justifies exclusion only by analogy ('similar to the mean-field energy shift in other polaron problems') without checking that the supersolid band-edge energy has the same physical interpretation as the constant g_IB n_0 shift in the homogeneous BEC case.
technical_objections.objection: The mean-field-like energy shift of the lowest impurity Bloch band is excluded from the definition of polaronic energy by analogy with flat-BEC polaron conventions. In the supersolid, this shift includes the impurity interacting with the periodic condensate density and depends on the same g_IB that drives the Fröhlich vertex; the separation is not as clean, and excluding it may conflate the impurity's kinetic and interaction energetics.

technical_objections.evidence: Paper states 'the divergence of the number of excitations dressing the impurity will result in a large local depletion where Bogoliubov theory used here will fail.' The experimental-signature claim is therefore at most qualitative.
technical_objections.objection: The divergence of the polaronic energy at the phase transition is presented as a potential experimental signature of supersolidity, but the underlying Bogoliubov theory is acknowledged to fail precisely at the divergence. The paper also provides no estimate of finite-size, trap, temperature, or lifetime effects that would determine experimental observability.
technical_objections.paper_location: Sec. I (abstract, intro) and Sec. IV discussion of Fig. 2
